coredata.dc:title: Mitochondrial nanoprobe for precise cellular and drug analysis via graph Neural network

coredata.dc:creator: He, Hua
Qin, Guangyong
Xue, Minmin
Feng, Zhenzhen
Mao, Jian
Tao, Wenpeng
Chen, Hongqi
Wang, Xiaojuan
Yu, Daoyong
Huang, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Mitochondrial morphology is crucial for cell identification and drug toxicity evaluation, yet it is challenged by mitochondrial complexities and limitations in imaging technologies. We develop an ultrasmall (∼1.5 nm) fluorescent carbon dot (CD), specifically designed to target mitochondria in live cells. These CDs exhibit near-infrared fluorescence at 685 nm, enhancing fluorescence imaging’s signal-to-noise ratio by 2–3 times. Their dense-blinking behavior enables rapid fluctuation-based super-resolution imaging with as few as 10 frames, thereby allowing for detailed visualization of mitochondrial morphology and dynamics. We further propose a graph-based deep learning framework that integrates multidimensional mitochondrial features, which are updated using a Graph Neural Network (GNN), to achieve precise mitochondria-based cellular typing and drug toxicity analysis with up to 98% accuracy. Additionally, we pioneer the use of interpretability algorithms to elucidate the GNN model, revealing how the depicted mitochondrial features by the CDs drive these predictions. This approach has significant implications in cellular and toxicological research, offering a unique tool for deciphering cellular behaviors and drug interactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mitochondria
Carbon Dots
Super-Resolution
Graph Neural Network
Toxicity Evaluation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and synthesis of mitochondrial CDs
Probing mitochondrial morphology and dynamics for cellular typing
Predicting mitochondrial toxicity across different drugs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of CDs for mitochondrial imaging. (A) Illustration of the synthetic route for creating CDs. (B) TEM image showcasing the morphology of CDs. (C) UV–Vis spectra of CDs, with an inset detailing their optical absorption in the defined spectral region outlined by a blue square. (D) Fluorescence spectra of CDs. (E) Visualization of HOMO and LUMO molecular orbitals for lysine-based polymer condensates with rhodamine-like structure. (F) Confocal and (H) wide-field fluorescence imaging of 4T1 cells stained with CDs (left) and MTR (right), excited by 635-nm and 532-nm lasers, respectively. (G) Scatter plot correlating pixel intensities from the fluorescence images in panel F, indicating a colocalization coefficient of 0.91. (I) SNR comparison between cells stained with CDs and MTR, observed under confocal and wide-field microscopy. (J) Quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity for CDs and MTR across varying concentrations. (K) Fluorescence response of CDs and MTR in the presence and absence of H2O2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Enhanced mitochondrial imaging through charge-driven blinking of CDs. (A) Fluorescence images of CDs excited with a 635-nm laser at an intensity of 150 W/cm2. (B) Four representative fluorescence trajectories of single CDs. (C) Schematic mechanism for the CD blinking. Comparisons of average values for (D) blinking cycles (n), (E) photon outputs (N), (F) on-times (τ on) and (G) off-times (τ off) of single CDs at solutions of pH 3, 7, and 11. (H) Sequential representation from left to right: Wide-field, time-averaged (over 10 frames), TRA (over 10 frames) and TRAC (over 20 frames) image of CD-stained mitochondria in 4T1 cells. Exposure time: 100 ms per frame. (I) Fluorescence intensity line profiles corresponding to identical regions in their respective images, as indicated by the white lines in panel H.

Probing mitochondrial morphology and dynamics for cellular classification using GNN. (A) TRA image showcasing CD-stained mitochondria in 4T1 cells. (B) Magnified view of a specific area highlighted by a dashed box in panel a. (C, D) Binarized images derived from the TRA image, depicting mitochondrial morphology (C) at the initial moment and (D) after a 10-second interval. Blue and red plus signs indicate centroid shifts in mitochondria over the 10-second duration. (E, G) Representative TRA images and (F, H) binarized images of a small region (marked by a dashed box) of CD-stained mitochondria in HeLa (E) and 293A (G) cells. (I) Geometric modelling of mitochondria in a confined cell region, with nodes representing morphology and dynamics per frame in TRA sequences and edges linking temporally proximate detections. Each node encapsulates mitochondrial features like intensity, area, major axis, minor axis, equivalent diameter, eccentricity, and movement rate, with edges denoting connections between nodes. (J) Schematic of the network architecture incorporating a sequence of GCN layers for node embedding learning, succeeded by fully connected layers for feature transformation, a Readout layer to aggregate these embeddings into a graph-level representation, culminating in output layers designated for classification. (K) Comparative prediction accuracies of various machine learning models determined via 5-fold cross-validation. (L) Shapley value summary plot for HeLa cell classification, displaying each node feature in the test samples, with the color encoding the feature’s value. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Predictive analysis of mitochondrial toxicity induced by various drugs. (A) TRA images of CD-stained mitochondria in 4T1 cells following incubation with rotenone, CCCP, and valinomycin, displayed sequentially from left to right. (B) Confusion matrix illustrating an average prediction accuracy of 98%. (C) Assessment of feature importance using Shapley values, accompanied by a summary plot (C) for 4T1 cells without drug incubation. Analysis of feature importance using Shapley values in 4T1 cells incubated with (D) rotenone, (E) CCCP, and (F) valinomycin. Dependence plots showing the impact of the most influential feature on the model’s predictions, in relation to the strongest interacting feature, for (G) rotenone, (H) CCCP, and (I) valinomycin.

Time-Dependent Mitochondrial Toxicity Prediction. (A) Time-lapse TRA images of 4T1 cells with CD-stained mitochondria post-rotenone incubation at 0, 3, 6, 10, 14, and 24 h, arranged sequentially. (B) A confusion matrix depicts the model's average prediction accuracy at 92%. (C-H) Shapley value-based feature importance analysis across the mentioned time points. (I-K) Dependence plots elucidate the relationship between the most significant feature and its primary interacting counterpart at 3, 10, and 24 h of incubation.

head.ce:title: Mitochondrial nanoprobe for precise cellular and drug analysis via graph Neural network

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing and College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing and College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mitochondrial morphology is crucial for cell identification and drug toxicity evaluation, yet it is challenged by mitochondrial complexities and limitations in imaging technologies. We develop an ultrasmall (∼1.5 nm) fluorescent carbon dot (CD), specifically designed to target mitochondria in live cells. These CDs exhibit near-infrared fluorescence at 685 nm, enhancing fluorescence imaging’s signal-to-noise ratio by 2–3 times. Their dense-blinking behavior enables rapid fluctuation-based super-resolution imaging with as few as 10 frames, thereby allowing for detailed visualization of mitochondrial morphology and dynamics. We further propose a graph-based deep learning framework that integrates multidimensional mitochondrial features, which are updated using a Graph Neural Network (GNN), to achieve precise mitochondria-based cellular typing and drug toxicity analysis with up to 98% accuracy. Additionally, we pioneer the use of interpretability algorithms to elucidate the GNN model, revealing how the depicted mitochondrial features by the CDs drive these predictions. This approach has significant implications in cellular and toxicological research, offering a unique tool for deciphering cellular behaviors and drug interactions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mitochondria, often referred to as the cellular powerhouses, are crucial organelles involved in energy generation, metabolism, apoptosis, and cellular signaling. Disruptions in mitochondrial function can significantly impact cellular health, as evidenced by alterations in their morphology and dynamics [1–3]. These changes make mitochondrial analysis integral to cellular health assessment, essential in pharmaceutical development and toxicity evaluation [4–6]. While electron microscopy provides detailed insights into mitochondrial ultrastructure, its complex preparation limits observations to single-time-point snapshots, hindering continuous, real-time monitoring of mitochondrial changes [7,8]. Fluorescence microscopy, a more accessible technique, facilitates observation of mitochondrial morphology and assessment of membrane potential [9]. Recent advancements in super-resolution methodologies, including Stimulated Emission Depletion Microscopy (STED) [10], Structured Illumination Microscopy (SIM) [11], Stochastic Optical Reconstruction Microscopy (STORM) [12], and fluctuation-based methods like Super-resolution Optical Fluctuation Imaging (SOFI) [13], and Super Resolution Radial Fluctuations (SRRF) [14], have enabled comprehensive analysis of dynamic mitochondrial networks [15–17]. Despite these technological advancements, the complex nature of mitochondrial morphology still presents significant challenges in its use as a biomarker for the accurate identification and analysis of mitochondrial status.

Applications of super-resolution imaging requires appropriate fluorescent probes for high-quality imaging of living cells [18,19]. Although certain dyes such as MitoTracker Red (MTR), Rhodamine 123, and teramethylrhodamine ethyl ester (TMRE) have proven effective for mitochondrial staining, they are susceptible to photobleaching under the intense illumination used in STED microscopy [20,21], and lack the photoswitchable properties required for STORM [19]. The limited spontaneous blinking characteristics of these dyes are also inadequate for rapid live-cell imaging techniques like dSTORM, SOFI, and SRRF [13,14,22]. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising alternative due to their high photostability, biocompatibility, and customizable structure for biological imaging, including mitochondria [23–29]. While some CDs exhibit blinking behaviors beneficial for fluctuation-based super-resolution imaging [25,30,31], their sparse spontaneous blinking hampers the ability to rapidly track mitochondria in live cells. Consequently, it becomes essential to develop a fluorescent probe characterized by dense blinking cycles over a short period to improve the temporal resolution of imaging.

Furthermore, the task of accurately analyzing and quantifying the morphology of mitochondria in live cells poses significant challenges [32,33]. Mitochondria, even within cells of the same type, display considerable variability, a situation further complicated by alterations in their orientation and dynamics during the imaging process. This variability compromises the assessment of mitochondrial or cellular physiological states, requiring extensive statistical analysis of numerous fluorescence images to discern mitochondrial morphologies across cells [34]. To enhance the accuracy of cell typing and drug efficacy evaluation, a technique capable of determining mitochondrial status from individual cell samples is imperative. In this context, a machine learning model, trained on extensive datasets, offers a promising solution for categorizing mitochondria in single samples. The success of this approach largely depends on the model's design, especially its ability to effectively extract features and perform classifications [35,36]. Recent developments in machine learning have improved the identification and classification of mitochondria by exploiting their complex features [36–38]. However, the practical application of these techniques in determining cell status and evaluating drug toxicity remains limited by challenges related to imaging resolution and the complex morphology and dynamics of mitochondria.

Herein, we develop ultrasmall (∼1.5 nm) CDs endowed with dense blinking properties, specifically designed for mitochondrial targeting in live cells. These CDs facilitate rapid, fluctuation-based super-resolution imaging, achieving high-quality reconstructions from as few as 10 frames. This capability allows for detailed analysis of mitochondrial morphology and dynamics. By integrating complex mitochondrial features with a graph neural network (GNN), we achieve a remarkable 98% accuracy in cellular identification and toxicity prediction. The model’s explainable algorithms provide crucial insights into its decision-making processes, marking a significant advancement in cellular and toxicology research.

CDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted method. Initially, 114 mg of L-lysine and 74 mg of 8-hydroxydolonidine were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ). This solution was then transferred into a sealed digestion vessel placed within a microwave digestion furnace. Upon exposure to microwave irradiation, the reaction mixture rapidly reached temperatures above 100 °C and was maintained at 160 °C for 2 h. After completion of the reaction, the mixture was allowed to gradually cool to room temperature. The resulting product was filtered through a membrane filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm. Subsequently, it underwent dialysis using a 500 Da cutoff membrane to remove any aggregates and unreacted precursors. The production yield was determined to be approximately 20.4%. Finally, the purified CDs were freeze-dried for further characterization.

All optical characterizations were conducted at room temperature, approximately 25 °C. FTIR spectra were obtained using a NICOLET 6700 IR spectrometer from Thermo Scientific. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra and lifetime were measured with a FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (UK). For Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analyses, 1H and 13C NMR spectra were acquired using a Bruker Avance III 600 MHz spectrometer. TEM images were captured using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV to examine the morphology of the CDs.

Theoretical calculations were conducted using the Gaussian 16 W software program. Geometric optimization and quantum chemical calculations for each molecule were performed at the B3LYP/6–311 + G (d, p) level employing Density Functional Theory (DFT). Visualization of the calculated results were accomplished using GaussView 6.0.

CDs were coated onto pristine coverslips from a dilute aqueous solution, ensuring a controlled density of approximately 1 particle/μm2. The CDs was excited by a 635 nm laser under a Nikon total internal reflection fluorescence microscopy (TIRFM), and the resultant fluorescence was collected through a 100 × objective (NA = 1.49) with an integrated 1.5 × magnification changer lens, a 640 nm dichroic mirror (DM), and a 693/40 nm band-pass filter, before detection by an Electron Multiplying Charge-Coupled Device (EMCCD; iXon 897, Andor, 16 μm/pixel). The EMCCD's gain and read-out rate were configured at 300 and 10 MHz, respectively. Fluorescence intensity was transmuted into photon counts using the electrons/Analog Digital Units (ADU) conversion factor (DCF), EM gain, and quantum efficiency (QE), with the relation: photons = ADU × DCF / (QE × gain), where DCF equaled 12.02 and QE approximated 92% for 600–750 nm fluorescence. The data was analyzed using a custom MATLAB program, extracting single molecule signals from a 7 × 7-pixel region centered around peak intensity. Fluorescence blinking events were discerned by isolating signals exceeding four times the standard deviation of background fluorescence trajectory fluctuations.

All cell lines were purchased from Shanghai Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Human cervical carcinoma Hela and human embryonic kidney 293A cells were cultured in high Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), murine mammary carcinoma 4T1 cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium. These mediums were supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% Penicillin-Streptomycin solution, and all cell lines were maintained in a humidified incubator at 37℃ with 5% CO2. To stain live cells, 200 μL CDs staining solution was added into the chamber after the removal of the old medium, and then incubated at 37 ℃ for 2 h. After incubation, the cells were washed three times with their corresponding medium and then imaged by fluorescence microscopy. CD-stained cells were imaged using TIRFM. The imaging conditions were similar to those used in single-molecule imaging, except for using an angle slightly smaller than the critical angle. This modification enabled imaging to a depth of 1–2 μm into the cytoplasm. Confocal fluorescence images were captured using a Nikon A1 confocal laser scanning system (Nikon, Japan) under a 100 × oil objective. Imaging was conducted using a 532 nm laser with emission collected between 552–617 nm, and a 635 nm laser with emission collected between 662–737 nm. The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the fluorescence images was determined by dividing the average intensity of the signal by the standard deviation of the background [39]

Rotenone, carbonyl cyanide 3-chlorophenylhydrazone (CCCP), and valinomycin were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). These compounds were initially dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and subsequently diluted in RPMI-1640 medium to achieve concentrations at or below their half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) values: 1 μM for rotenone, 10 μM for CCCP, and 10 nM for valinomycin. Approximately 600 4T1 cells were seeded into a Labtek Eight-well Chamber and grown until 80% confluence. Subsequently, the cells were treated with 200 μL of the respective drug solutions and incubated for 3, 6, 10, 14, and 24 h. Following the drug treatment, cells were stained with CDs for 2 h and then washed three times with RPMI-1640 medium to remove excess stain prior to imaging.

Fluorescence image stacks were captured with a resolution of 512 × 512 pixels, at 14 bits per pixel, and a capture rate of 10 MHz. Each frame had an exposure time of 100 ms. SRRF images were generated using an open-source Python script provided in the literature [40]. The settings for image construction included a ring radius of 1.6, a magnification factor of 5, an axes ring of 6, and a TRAC order of 2. For TRA imaging, the order was set to 0, with other parameters left at default values. Mitochondrial features such as intensity, area, major axis length, minor axis width, equivalent diameter, eccentricity, and velocity were quantified from TRA images using a custom-developed MATLAB program.

For each cell type or cell status, 500 to 1500 ROIs, each measuring 500 × 500 × 6 pixels, were cropped from TRA image sequences. ROIs containing fewer than 3 mitochondria or with a total area smaller than 50 pixels after binarization and removal of regions connected to the edges were excluded. Features of mitochondria within each ROI were extracted and averaged using a MATLAB program. These feature values were used to construct a graph dataset in the format compatible with the PyTorch Geometric (PyG) library. This dataset included node indices, features, edge indices, and graph object labels. The dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets, allocated in the proportions of 80%, 10%, and 10%, respectively. The dataset was loaded using PyG with a batch size of 16 and inputted into the GNN model. The GNN model was developed using the open-source packages PyTorch 1.13 and Python 3.9 and trained on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2060 Super GPU. Training was conducted over 1000 epochs using the train-validate-test splitting method. The learning rate was set at 0.001, and the Adam optimizer was employed. The model underwent a 5-fold cross-validation procedure to assess its robustness.

The model explanation was conducted using GNNExplainer [41] and CaptumExplainer [42], both implemented in the PyG library. GNNExplainer was employed to quantify the weight of each feature in a node and the significance of each edge in the context of specific cases. CaptumExplainer, a wrapper around the Captum library, encompasses various attribution methods to elucidate any PyG model. In this study, the Shapley Value sampling algorithm was employed to estimate Shapley values from the test dataset, thereby offering a clear understanding of each feature's contribution to the model's predictions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and synthesis of mitochondrial CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesis of CDs is achieved through dehydration and condensation reactions between lysine and 8-hydroxydolonidine, under microwave-assisted heating (Fig. 1
A). In this process, lysine facilitates the formation of polymer-like nanoparticles via cross-linking, imbuing them with numerous positive charges to augment mitochondrial affinity. Simultaneously, 8-hydroxydolonidine contributes to the development of a rhodamine-like structure [25,43], crucial for the targeted mitochondrial specificity of CDs. Subsequently, these CDs were characterized comprehensively to confirm their structure and effectiveness in binding to mitochondria in live cells. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images demonstrate that the CDs exhibit a uniform and ultra-small size (Fig. 1 B), averaging around 1.5 nm in diameter. Additional images showcasing a broader field of view and the size distribution are provided in Fig. S1 . Agarose gel electrophoresis reveals that the CDs possess an overall positive net charge, showing consistent mass-to-charge ratios (Fig. S2). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra further identifies an abundance of –NH2 groups in the CDs (Fig. S3), supporting the findings from the electrophoresis analysis. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis indicates that the CDs are primarily composed of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O), with the presence of C-C/C = C, C-N = C, C-N-C, and N-C3/N-H bonds, signifying nitrogen-enriched CDs (Fig. S4). NMR spectra of 13C and 1H confirm the presence of an aromatic ring structure with sp2 hybridization in the CDs (Fig. S5). In alignment with previously documented CDs [44–46], the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of our CDs features distinct absorption peaks around 260 and 405 nm (Fig. 1 C). These peaks correspond to the π-π* transition of the aromatic C = C bond and the n-π* transition within N- and O-doped structures, respectively. A specific absorption peak at approximately 650 nm in the CDs indicates the creation of additional electronic energy levels owing to the involution of rhodamine-like structure the CD surface (Fig. 1 C inset). Upon excitation with 635 nm light, these CDs exhibit near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence peaked at 685 nm (Fig. 1 D), with a lifetime of 1.9 ns, significantly differing from the 4.2 ns of the CD core under 405 nm excitation (Fig. S6). The fluorescence excitation spectrum for CDs, with the emission wavelength fixed at 685 nm reveals a pronounced excitation band spanning 550–650 nm (Fig. S7). The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs, estimated using Alexa Fluor 647 (known QY of 33%) as a reference, is approximately 5%. In addition, we analyzed the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO-LUMO) energies across various lysine-based polymers and their condensates with 8-hydroxydolonidine (Table S1). We observed that both polymerization and condensation with 8-hydroxydolonidine narrow the HOMO-LUMO energy gap (ΔE), with the effect being more pronounced in the latter case (Fig. S8). The ΔE value of the resultant aromatic polymers can decrease to 1.919 eV (∼646 nm). The distribution of HOMO and LUMO orbitals as illustrated in Fig. 1 E indicates effective delocalization between the lysine-based backbone and the rhodamine-like structure.

Confocal fluorescence imaging confirmed the ability of the CDs to effectively accumulated into mitochondria in live cells, with a colocalization coefficient of 0.91 with MTR (Fig. 1 F, G). This enables observation of mitochondrial dynamics under wide-field mode (Fig. 1 H, Fig. S1 ). In both imaging modalities, the CDs yield a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) 2 ∼ 3 times higher than that achieved with MTR (Fig. 1 I). Additionally, unlike MTR, the CDs exhibit aggregation-enhanced emission (AIE) and heightened resistance to hydrogen peroxide (Fig. 1 J, K). These fluorescent characteristics are particularly advantageous, as conventional fluorescent dyes are susceptible to oxidative stress and frequently experience aggregation-induced quenching, both of which pose challenges in the detection of mitochondria [47,48]. When treating cells with paraformaldehyde (PFA) prior to CD staining, mitochondria remained undetectable, indicating that CD-mediated staining depends on membrane potential (Fig. S9). Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of CDs against different cell lines was assessed by MTT assays. The results reveal that the cells maintain a viability > 96% after 24-hour incubation with CDs at concentrations up to 200 μg / mL, indicating their low cytotoxicity (Fig. S10). These results underscore the potential of the CDs as robust probes for mitochondrion-specific fluorescence imaging.

The fluorescence of individual CDs was investigated using total internal reflection fluorescence microscopy (TIRFM), revealing bright emission and distinctive fluorescent blinking under a 635-nm laser (Fig. 2
A, B,
Fig. S2 ). The blinking observed in CDs is commonly attributed to the transfer of photoexcited electrons to surface trap states [49]. It has been established that surfaces functionalized with amino groups exhibit increased blinking cycles [30,50]. In this study, the CDs, synthesized from lysine, feature a surface abundant in amino groups. This characteristic influences the transition rates between on and off states (Fig. 2 C), with average rates of on-to-off (k d) and off-to-on (k b) at 4.17 s−1 and 0.89 s−1, respectively, in a pH 7 solution. Additionally, the protonation of the amino groups likely enhances their ability to act as electron acceptors, facilitating more frequent electron transfers between the carbon dot core and surface states, thereby increasing the CDs' blinking frequency. As demonstrated in Fig. 2 D-G, Figs. S11 , and S12, the blinking behavior shows pronounced pH sensitivity, with lower pH values significantly increasing blinking cycles, photon emission, and on-state duration, while reducing off-state duration. This ultimately results in enhanced ensemble fluorescence under acidic conditions, as illustrated in Fig. S13. Comparative analysis of the blinking properties of CDs with MTR and Cy5 (a NIR dye) at pH 7.0 disclosed that CDs exhibit an average blinking frequency of 28 cycles per minute, in contrast to 6 and 20 cycles per minute for MTR and Cy5, respectively (Fig. S14). The dense-blinking property is beneficial for rapid live-cell super-resolution imaging that rely on fluorescence fluctuations. To demonstrate this capability, we used the SRRF technique for mitochondrial imaging, as shown in Fig. 2 H. SRRF is a super-resolution algorithm that does not require the detection and localization of fluorophores. Instead, it employs a sophisticated analysis method that processes images based on radial symmetry. Fluctuations in signal over time are translated into changes in radiality, allowing for the differentiation between noise and actual signal through temporal analysis. This analysis was conducted using two approaches: temporal radiality average (TRA) and temporal radiality auto-correlations (TRAC) [14]. These approaches significantly improve resolution, as evidenced by the intensity line profiles in Fig. 2 I. TRA efficiently delineates mitochondrial boundaries with as few as 10 frames, enabling precise segmentation and tracking ( Fig. S3 ). Conversely, TRAC, though necessitating more images, boosts spatial resolution for revealing complex mitochondrial structures (Fig. S15). In contrast, SRRF imaging with MTR staining (Fig. S16), despite utilizing identical techniques, results in lower resolution compared to the enhanced clarity achieved with CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing mitochondrial morphology and dynamics for cellular typing
ce:section.ce:para:
TRA imaging with CDs was employed to probe mitochondrial morphology and dynamics across various cell lines, including 4T1, HeLa, and 293A cells (Fig. 3
A-H). Corresponding wide-field images are presented in Fig. S17. These images displayed cell-specific mitochondrial morphologies. Quantitative assessment of mitochondrial features, such as intensity, area, and shape parameters, was performed through image binarization. Mitochondrial motility was assessed by tracking centroid movements, as illustrated in Fig. 3 C, D. Each parameter was derived as an average value from a defined 10 × 10 μm2 region of interest (ROI). Statistical analysis over 500 ROIs per cell line revealed significant variances (p < 0.05) in mitochondrial characteristics among these cell lines (Fig. S18 A-G). For instance, regarding the major axis dimension, the sequence was observed as 4T1 > HeLa > 293A, whereas, in terms of motility, the order was reversed, with 4T1 < HeLa < 293A. Despite the distinct morphological and dynamical patterns observed, the complexity of mitochondrial features within each cell line posed challenges for cell type discrimination using a single ROI. An attempt to cluster and differentiate the cell types using the t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) method resulted in overlapping regions among the cell lines in the classification space (Fig. S18 H).

To probe detailed mitochondrial characteristics in a single region, our analysis involved examining successive frames of this region. We calculated the mean values of each feature for every frame as depicted in Fig. 3 I. The features, which are interconnected temporally and spatially, provide intricate details crucial for distinguishing mitochondria at various cellular statuses. A graph is constructed to integrate mitochondrial features from a 10 × 10 μm2 region over six consecutive TRA images (Fig. 3 J). Each graph object contains six nodes and fifteen edges, with each node embodying seven features: intensity, area, equivalent diameter, eccentricity, major axis, minor axis, and movement rate. The movement rate at each node is calculated by the average value across frames connected to that node, and edges denote the temporal relationships between frames. This graph object is fed into a GNN model [51], which then integrates the node features to classify cell status. Fig. S19 A, B illustrates the accuracy and loss curves during training and validation for the classification of 4T1, HeLa, and 293A cells. The confusion matrix demonstrates an average accuracy of 98% through 5-fold cross-validation (Fig. S19 C). In contrast, using conventional machine learning algorithms like Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) with inputs from average features of single and six-frame images resulted in a maximum accuracy of approximately 80% (Fig. 3 K and
Fig. S20 ). The superior performance of the GNN model in cellular typing illustrates its exceptional ability to integrate mitochondrial features. Unlike traditional algorithms, GNNs enhance feature representations by capturing both local and broader contextual information across multiple layers. This process involves updating features based on aggregated information from neighboring nodes, enabling the incorporation of relational features that may be overlooked by other methods.

To assess the impact of specific features on classification outcomes, we used two independent methods, GNNexplainer and Shapley Value sampling, to explain the GNN model. This dual-method enabled cross-validation of the interpretability results, ensuring robustness in our analysis (Figs. S21-23). The GNNexplainer elucidates the model's decision-making by allocating importance scores to each node feature and edge [41]. The Shapley Value sampling estimates Shapley values for each feature, quantifying its marginal contribution to the prediction across all possible feature combinations [52,53]. The results show that mitochondrial features universally contribute to cellular classification, but with varying degrees of importance depending on cell types. In identifying the most important features, the two algorithms gave almost identical results. Crucially, the Shapley-based method considers both positive and negative impacts of each feature on the model's output. For instance, a Shapley summary plot (Fig. 3 L) for HeLa cell classification demonstrates that increased minor and major mitochondrial axes are predictive of HeLa cells, while larger areas and diameters are indicative of non-HeLa cell classifications (Fig. S21). Further analysis depicted in Figs. S22 and S23 shows that an increase in both diameter and area, coupled with a reduction in the minor axes, tends to predict 4T1 cells. In contrast, an increase primarily in the area is more predictive of 293A cells. In addition, node features in the GNN model are integrated through edge connections. The two methods reveal that all edges in the graph object participate in feature integration with significant weights, which highlight the validity of our geometric modeling strategy. Together, these results demonstrate the GNN model's efficacy in cell typing and its interpretability for mitochondrion-based cell classification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Predicting mitochondrial toxicity across different drugs
ce:section.ce:para:
We used the CDs and GNN model to probe mitochondrial toxicity to various drugs and elucidate their differences in mitochondrial responses. We selected three representative drugs known to induce mitochondrial dysfunction: rotenone [54,55], CCCP [56], and valinomycin [57–59], each applied for a 6-hour incubation with 4T1 cells. Representative regions of TRA images are shown in Fig. 4
A, with TRA, binarized, and full wide-field images accessible in Fig. S24. Through extracting CDs-depicting mitochondrial features, we trained the GNN model for the classification of cell status. The accuracy of our model, as illustrated by the confusion matrix in Fig. 4 B, reaches 98% in discerning cells treated with each specific drug. This high accuracy not only confirms the utility of our method in predicting mitochondrial toxicity but also reveals the unique impacts of each drug on mitochondrial morphology and dynamics. The analysis presented in Fig. 4 C demonstrates that, in normal 4T1 cells, mitochondrial area and diameter are primary positive contributors, whereas the minor and major axes negatively influence predictions. Upon drug treatment, these relationships shift, as evidenced by Fig. 4 D-F. Specifically, rotenone treatment leads to decreased mitochondrial area and increased minor axis and eccentricity, indicative of smaller, irregular mitochondria, reflecting its inhibitory effect on the mitochondrial electron transfer chain and subsequent promotion of fission or autophagy [55,60]. In contrast, CCCP treatment enhances the major axis and diameter and decrease intensity, suggesting elongated mitochondria with reduced membrane potential, in line with CCCP's membrane depolarizing action [56]. Valinomycin treatment, marked by changes in the major and minor axes, suggests a shift towards smaller, rounder mitochondria, reflecting its function as a potassium ion carrier disrupting ionic balance [59]. These results underline the distinct mitochondrial morphological changes induced by each drug, corresponding to their unique mechanisms of action, and highlight the utility of Shapley values in understanding these effects. In addition, the Shapley summary plots (Fig. S25) reveal that the feature value magnitude does not linearly correlate with their Shapley values, suggesting complex interactions among mitochondrial features for model predictions. This is further detailed in Shapley value dependence plots. Fig. 4 G-I show the dependence of the primary feature on its strongest interacting features for contributing to Shapley values in drug-treated mitochondria. For rotenone, the minor axis emerges as the primary feature, mainly interacting with the diameter, suggesting mitochondrial enlargement. In contrast, the major axis is significant for CCCP and valinomycin treatments, with the diameter and area being the key interacting features, respectively. These findings elucidate distinct mitochondrial morphological responses to each drug, enhancing our comprehension of their mechanisms and informing toxicity assessments in drug development.

We finally used the CDs and GNN model for probing the mitochondrial toxicity of drugs over various incubation periods. Representative TRA images of 4T1 cell mitochondria treated with rotenone over time intervals ranging from 3 to 24 h are shown in Fig. 5
A, with additional images in Fig. S26. The model's accuracy, depicted in the confusion matrix (Fig. 5 B), stands at 92% for all classes across different exposure durations. This accuracy is slightly lower than that achieved in predicting cells types or various drug treatments because of the gradually induced mitochondrial toxicity against drugs. Notably, the prediction accuracy for cells exposed to rotenone for 3 h was about 88%, with a 5% misclassification rate for untreated cells, improving to 97% after 24 h, indicating pronounced morphological changes. Shapley value analysis (Fig. 5 C-H and Fig. S27 ) reveals the varying contributions of mitochondrial features to cell status classification over time, highlighting the dynamic nature of mitochondrial responses to prolonged drug exposure. The major axis of mitochondria consistently appeared as a key positive feature at 3, 10, and 24 h, influencing classification accuracy in conjunction with its diameter (Fig. 5 I-K). Particularly at the 24-hour, the diameter plays a dual role as a significant negative contributor and a critical interactive feature. These complex variations and interdependencies among features constitute the foundational elements enabling the model to discern cells across diverse durations of drug exposure. In addition, applying this methodology to cells treated with CCCP produced similar predictive outcomes (Figs. S28, 29), underscoring the technique's effectiveness in identifying mitochondrial toxicity and its temporal variations at the single-cell level.

We have developed a NIR fluorescent CD engineered to target mitochondria in live cells, enhancing fluorescence imaging's SNR by two to three times. These CDs, with their dense blinking properties, enable rapid super-resolution imaging, thus facilitating precise monitoring of mitochondrial morphology and dynamics. To harness these imaging advancements, we devised a graph structure that integrates complex mitochondrial features, which were refined through a GNN model to achieve high accuracy up to 98% in cellular typing and drug toxicity analysis. Our interpretability analysis of the GNN model unveils the contribution of specific mitochondrial features illuminated by CDs to these high-precision predictions. This synergy between CDs-based mitochondrial imaging and graph deep learning not only advances our understanding of cell identity and drug toxicity but also holds significant potential for cellular biology and pharmacology research, offering a new dimension to the study of mitochondrial dynamics and their impact on cellular functions.

Hua He: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Guangyong Qin: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Minmin Xue: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhenzhen Feng: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Jian Mao: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wenpeng Tao: Validation, Resources, Data curation. Hongqi Chen: Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiaojuan Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Formal analysis. Daoyong Yu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Fang Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2023MB010, ZR2022MB016), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874154, 22177133), and the Research Project of General Administration of China Customs (2022HK058).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.152709.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
